notes: Exhaustive regression suite for crux trigger behavior, source-backed grounding, layered-pipeline fidelity, cohesive output, strategy-frame repair, dao/qi internal reasoning, and read-only boundaries.

evals.prompt: Use design:crux to find the crux in this plan: we should add AI recommendations, rebuild onboarding, and create a premium dashboard so power users trust us.
evals.expected_output: Compresses the compound strategy into load-bearing claims, converts loaded terms into behavior, and names the deciding assumption.
assertions: Does not audit every feature as a separate framework section.
Names the weak joint behind trust, premium, and power users.
Ends with one crux question or a decisive next test.

evals.prompt: Use crux. Ask me questions about this idea: an AI copilot that writes release notes from engineering work.
evals.expected_output: Asks only the highest-pressure questions and avoids a long questionnaire.
assertions: Includes no more than two supporting questions plus one final crux question.
Questions convert loaded terms into behavior or evidence.
Does not answer the whole product strategy before exposing the crux.

evals.prompt: This onboarding should feel premium, simple, and more impactful.
evals.expected_output: Translates style words into observable mechanics and behavior without treating them as one standard.
assertions: Splits premium, simple, and impactful into different possible standards.
Uses interface mechanics such as hierarchy, density, choices, first success, risk, or confidence.
Names the weak joint instead of giving generic taste advice.

evals.prompt: I want to build an AI writing assistant for designers.
evals.expected_output: Names the solution-first weak joint and asks whether the writing moment matters beyond generic clean prose.
assertions: Identifies the painful writing moment as unproven.
Compares against generic ChatGPT or clean writing as the baseline.
Asks a crux question that would change whether the product is worth building.

evals.prompt: Context: our team is nervous because two large incumbents announced adjacent AI features. The board wants a strategy answer by Friday. The team keeps saying platform vs point solution, wide vs deep, and CAC vs LTV. Find the core.
evals.expected_output: Filters noise, refuses prestige abstraction, and moves one level down into customer, moment, capability, behavior, proof, and tradeoff.
assertions: Names the status-safe abstraction pattern.
Does not answer each binary as a separate disconnected section.
Gives better frames the team can use instead.

evals.prompt: Should we go wide or deep now that two incumbents entered our space?
evals.expected_output: Rejects the altitude of the binary before recommending any product shape.
assertions: First sentence names the weak frame, weak joint, or avoided reality.
Does not open by choosing wide, deep, narrow-deep, both, or it depends.
Does not recommend a shape unless it names an actual customer, moment, capability, behavior, and proof signal.
If facts are missing, says product shape should follow from the bet and offers sharper candidate bet questions.

evals.prompt: Find the crux of this strategy: platform vs point solution, CAC vs LTV, horizontal vs vertical, wide vs deep.
evals.expected_output: Collapses the category stack into the real bet instead of giving separate framework outputs.
assertions: Names what the stacked binaries let the room avoid.
Explains the core in simple language.
Gives reframes that force a concrete customer, behavior, proof, or tradeoff.

evals.prompt: We sell AI claim-review software to mid-market insurance teams. Should we go wide into underwriting and fraud, or go deep on adjuster review when incumbents enter?
evals.expected_output: Uses the supplied segment and moment to formulate a concrete bet before discussing product shape.
assertions: Names mid-market insurance teams and adjuster review.
Discusses underwriting or fraud only as consequences of the proof.
Ties any shape recommendation to commitment or behavior signals.

evals.prompt: Read plugins/design/skills/crux/evals/fixtures/risk-score-prd.md and find the crux.
evals.expected_output: Inspects the PRD, pressures trust and AI recommendations, and does not ask questions answerable from the file.
assertions: Mentions observed PRD facts.
Distinguishes source testimony from behavior evidence.
Includes a crude test with a legibility signal.

evals.prompt: Read plugins/design/skills/crux/evals/fixtures/cancellation-context.md and find the crux in my plan: accounts should be cancellable.
evals.expected_output: Uses the fixture vocabulary conflict as the weak joint instead of asking what account means.
assertions: Mentions the fixture's workspace/subscription terminology.
Names the contradiction between account and cancellable.
Replaces answerable questions with observed facts.

must_not_include_any: what do you mean by account

evals.prompt: Read plugins/design/skills/crux/evals/fixtures/refund-approval-conflict.md. Docs say admins approve refunds, code says support agents approve refunds, product says users approve refunds. Find the crux.
evals.expected_output: Surfaces actor ownership as the weak joint and classifies evidence without inventing behavior.
assertions: Names admins, support agents, and users as conflicting actors.
Frames the deciding question around decision rights and accountability.
Does not treat any one source as observed user behavior.

evals.prompt: Read plugins/design/skills/crux/evals/fixtures/missing-product-doc.md and find the crux.
evals.expected_output: Says the file cannot be read and does not invent observed evidence.
assertions: Explicitly reports the missing file.
Does not fabricate source pressure.
May still pressure the pasted claim if one exists, but does not claim artifact facts.

evals.prompt: Ask me questions about plugins/design/skills/crux/evals/fixtures/risk-score-prd.md.
evals.expected_output: Inspects the PRD first and asks only unresolved high-pressure questions.
assertions: Does not ask for facts explicitly present in the PRD.
Asks at most two supporting questions plus one final crux question.
Questions target behavior evidence or decision pressure.

evals.prompt: Find the crux of this plan: launch AI recommendations, rebuild onboarding, reduce support tickets, add a premium dashboard, and move enterprise users to annual plans.
evals.expected_output: Uses the layered pipeline internally and renders a single cohesive synthesis.
assertions: Compresses to the top load-bearing assumptions.
Does not expose all pipeline stages or method names.
Includes weak joint, specific bet pressure, and a small test.

evals.prompt: Use crux deeply on this: our AI assistant should replace the dashboard because conversational UX is more natural.
evals.expected_output: Applies method-agent pipeline semantics internally when available, but does not reveal method-agent artifacts.
assertions: Tests whether conversational action can carry monitoring, accountability, and repeated scanning.
Does not expose method-agent outputs or named lenses as sections.
Names the opposite truth: dashboards may still be better for some jobs.

evals.prompt: Use crux and explain the dao/qi distinction in this claim: the feature should look simpler so users trust it.
evals.expected_output: Because the user asks for the method, defines dao/qi briefly and applies it naturally.
assertions: Defines dao as invisible operating principle or causal model.
Defines qi as visible expression or artifact.
Uses the distinction to pressure whether visual simplicity actually creates trust behavior.

evals.prompt: This feature should look simpler so users trust it.
evals.expected_output: Uses dao/qi internally but does not expose named method sections.
assertions: Converts simpler and trust into behavior.
Names the weak joint.
Does not show dao/qi labels because the user did not ask for method.

evals.prompt: Pressure this idea: marketplace sellers need a premium analytics hub with AI insights, better onboarding, and lower support load.
evals.expected_output: Produces one connected line of thought, not disconnected framework blocks.
assertions: Paragraphs build on each other causally.
Does not create separate sections for every noun.
Ties analytics, AI insights, onboarding, and support to one deciding behavior or proof point.

evals.prompt: Find the crux: teams say they would love an AI meeting summary workspace.
evals.expected_output: Treats praise as weak evidence and designs a test around commitment.
assertions: Names testimony or praise as weak evidence.
Success signal requires behavior such as data access, repeated use, payment, invite, or replacement.
Includes failure signal and time to test.

evals.prompt: Find the crux: users need AI recommendations to approve high-risk payments faster.
evals.expected_output: Includes legibility pressure in the crude test because this is a product/design claim.
assertions: Names whether users understand what the recommendation means and expects.
Separates trust from speed.
Includes desirability, feasibility, viability, and legibility when giving a crude test.

evals.prompt: This dashboard should be simpler for power users.
evals.expected_output: Creates one scenario wedge that shows where power users behave like beginners or need density.
assertions: Does not treat power user as a stable persona without evidence.
Uses one concrete edge scenario.
Pressures whether simpler means fewer steps, better scanning, fewer choices, or faster recovery.

evals.prompt: Use crux to update the context doc as we decide terms.
evals.expected_output: Redirects the write by default and may identify documentation debt without editing docs.
assertions: Says crux is read-only by default or redirects to a writing workflow.
May name documentation debt.
Does not claim it updated a file.

evals.prompt: Find the recurring thinking error here and save it to memory: every time competitors move, we debate categories instead of customers.
evals.expected_output: Surfaces a compact memory candidate but does not persist it unless explicitly routed to memory-writing instructions.
assertions: Names the recurring assumption or blind spot.
Does not claim a memory was saved.
Keeps the answer focused on the crux.

evals.prompt: Rewrite this button label to be shorter: 'Proceed to final payment confirmation'.
evals.expected_output: Should route to copy/content design or answer directly, not run a crux interrogation.
assertions: Does not force weak joint/crude test/crux question.
Does not load crux just because the prompt contains a product surface.
If answered, gives a concise rewrite.

evals.prompt: Implement the onboarding screen in React with the attached design tokens.
evals.expected_output: Should route to frontend/dev skill, not crux.
assertions: Does not interrogate whether onboarding should exist.
Does not ask crux questions before implementation unless the user asks for strategy.
Should not edit files as part of crux.

evals.prompt: /dex release design patch
evals.expected_output: Should route to dex release workflow, not crux.
assertions: Does not pressure the strategy behind release.
Does not ask product questions.
Keeps release behavior unchanged.

evals.prompt: Grill this implementation plan against the docs and keep asking follow-up questions until the model is complete.
evals.expected_output: Should route to grill-with-docs when the user wants iterative domain modeling, not crux.
assertions: Does not absorb the docs-grilling workflow into crux.
Preserves crux as deciding-assumption work.
May hand off to the docs-grilling workflow.

evals.prompt: Should we go wide or deep?
evals.expected_output: Does not treat placeholder wording as a concrete bet.
assertions: Does not say a placeholder like one customer type is the specific bet.
Says more facts are needed or gives sharper questions that would expose the bet.
Does not recommend product shape from missing context.

evals.prompt: Now that incumbents entered, do we go broad, deep, or both?
evals.expected_output: Starts by naming the avoided reality, not by choosing a side.
assertions: Opening sentence does not choose a product shape.
Frames broad/deep/both as consequences.
Moves to customer, moment, capability, behavior, proof, and tradeoff.

evals.prompt: Read plugins/design/skills/crux/evals/fixtures/cancellation-context.md and ask me the crux questions for partial cancellation.
evals.expected_output: Reads the fixture before questions and avoids asking for terminology already present.
assertions: Mentions workspace and subscription facts before asking.
Asks only unresolved pressure questions.
Does not ask what terms mean when the fixture defines them.

evals.prompt: Use crux deeply on this: our AI assistant should replace the dashboard because conversational UX is more natural.
evals.expected_output: Pressures whether conversational UX can preserve accountability, decision rights, monitoring, comparison, auditability, and recovery.
assertions: Names accountability or decision rights explicitly.
Does not treat natural conversation as enough evidence.
Includes the opposite truth that dashboards remain better for some scanning or accountability jobs.

evals.prompt: Use crux to update the context doc as we decide terms.
evals.expected_output: Makes the read-only boundary visible before identifying documentation debt or routing to a writing workflow.
assertions: Says crux is read-only by default.
Does not claim the document was updated.
Names the deciding pressure or documentation debt.

evals.prompt: Find the crux and leave me with better ideas: we want AI to reduce support tickets for merchants.
evals.expected_output: Explains the core and expands from it into better research, product, or positioning directions.
assertions: Does not brainstorm generic AI support features from the original frame.
Opportunities follow from the specific core insight.
Keeps the summary simple enough to repeat.
